title: Technical Writing Clarity
subtitle: Make engineers want to read what you wrote.
markdown:
## Why this matters

Clear technical writing is the highest-leverage skill an engineer never learns. Your code can be perfect — but if your PR description is sloppy, your design doc rambles, or your bug report buries the lede, you waste your reviewer's time. Multiply that by every PR, every doc, every Slack message. Over a career, unclear writing is the single biggest tax on your output.

The good news: the patterns are mechanical. You can learn them in an afternoon and apply them for the rest of your career.

---

## The 3 patterns of clear technical writing

### 1. BLUF — Bottom Line Up Front

State your conclusion in the first sentence. Then justify it.

**Bad:**
> I was looking into the latency spike from yesterday and noticed that our cache hit rate dropped after the deploy. Looking at the logs more carefully, it seems like the new endpoint isn't using the cache layer at all because of how we initialize the client. So I think we should...

**Good:**
> **The new `/v2/orders` endpoint bypasses the cache.** Its client is initialized before the cache layer mounts, so every call goes straight to the DB. Fix: defer client init to after `app.ready`.

Reader knows the answer in 8 words. Everything after is evidence.

### 2. Inverted pyramid

Most important fact → supporting facts → details. Reader can stop at any point and have the gist.

```
[Conclusion]              ← everyone reads this
  [Why it matters]        ← decision-makers read this
    [How it works]        ← implementers read this
      [Edge cases]        ← reviewers read this
```

This is the opposite of how you discovered the answer. You found it last; you write it first.

### 3. Signposting

Tell the reader what's coming. Headings, numbered lists, bold key phrases. Long prose blocks die in technical contexts.

**Bad:** A wall of 4 paragraphs explaining your migration plan.

**Good:**
> ### Migration plan
> 1. **Backfill the new column** (online, ~6 hours)
> 2. **Switch reads** behind a feature flag (instant, reversible)
> 3. **Switch writes** after 1 week of cohabitation (one-way door)
> 4. **Drop the old column** (after 30 days of green metrics)

---

## Common failure modes

| Failure | Why it happens | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Hedging** ("It might possibly be...") | Fear of being wrong | State the claim. If wrong, you'll be corrected fast. Hedging slows everyone down. |
| **Passive voice** ("It was decided that...") | Avoiding ownership | Name the actor. "We decided" / "I propose". |
| **Jargon walls** | Showing off | Use the term, then a 5-word definition in parens. Optimize for the reader, not your image. |
| **Mystery context** ("Per our discussion...") | Assuming reader has it loaded | Quote the relevant fact inline. Don't make the reader chase a thread. |
| **Burying the ask** | Politeness theatre | Put the ask in the first 2 sentences. "I need approval to merge X by Friday because Y." |

---

## Worked example — a PR description rewrite

### The original (real, names changed)

> Hey team! So I was working on the issue from last week (#3421) and I think I have a fix. The problem was that when users with multiple sessions tried to log out, only one session was being killed. I traced it through the auth middleware and found that we were calling deleteSession with sessionId rather than userId. I changed it to use userId and added a test. Let me know what you think!

### What's wrong

- 4 sentences before the fix is named.
- "What you think" — vague ask. Approve? Comment? Bikeshed?
- No call-out of risk (does deleting all sessions log out the current request mid-flight?).
- No test plan beyond "added a test."

### The rewrite

> **Fixes #3421 — logout now kills all sessions for the user.**
>
> Was: `deleteSession(sessionId)` killed only the current session.
> Now: `deleteSessionsForUser(userId)` kills all of them.
>
> **Risk:** none — the current request completes (auth middleware reads the token before this runs).
>
> **Test:** new test in `auth.test.ts` covers the multi-session case. Manually verified by logging in on two browsers and clicking logout on one — both terminate.
>
> **Asking for:** review + merge. No follow-up needed.

Same information. Half the words. Reviewer knows in 5 seconds whether to approve.

---

## Mini-checklist (apply this to your next PR / doc / Slack message)

- [ ] **Conclusion in the first sentence.** If a tired reader stops there, do they have the gist?
- [ ] **Named the actor.** No passive voice unless the actor genuinely doesn't matter.
- [ ] **Stated the ask.** What do you need from the reader, by when?
- [ ] **Risks called out.** Even "no risk" is a useful sentence.
- [ ] **Headings if it's longer than 5 lines.** Reader's eye needs anchors.
- [ ] **Read it back as if you were the reader, not the writer.** Cut anything that isn't earning its place.

---

## When to practice

Every PR description. Every design doc. Every Slack message longer than 3 lines. Every postmortem. The skill compounds — within a quarter, your colleagues will notice, and within a year, it will be the most-cited reason for your promotion.

diagrams.caption: The inverted pyramid — most important fact at the top, details at the bottom
diagrams.source:
graph TD
    A[Conclusion / Bottom Line] --> B[Why it matters]
    B --> C[How it works]
    C --> D[Edge cases & details]
    style A fill:#0d9488,color:#fff,stroke:#0d9488
    style B fill:#5eead4,color:#134e4a,stroke:#0d9488
    style C fill:#ccfbf1,color:#134e4a,stroke:#5eead4
    style D fill:#f0fdfa,color:#134e4a,stroke:#5eead4

tutorOpener: Welcome — let's sharpen your technical writing. Tell me about a time you wrote something (PR description, doc, Slack message) that you wish you'd written more clearly. What was confusing for the reader, and why did it land that way?
